turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some pretty bad abdominal pain and bloody stools for about three weeks now, and I'm really worried it's not going away on its own. It's been getting worse over time, and I'm starting to feel like something serious is going on.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: The pain is crampy, mostly on my left side, and it feels like a constant burning sensation. Sometimes it's sharp and stabbing, but usually it's just this dull ache that I can't shake. The bloody stools are really what's got me worried, though - they're bright red and mixed in with the regular stool, not just black or tarry like when you eat something bad.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yeah, I'd say that's when things get really uncomfortable. Along with the pain and bloody stools, I've been having diarrhea a lot more often than usual - like 8 or 10 times a day, which is way up from my normal 1 or 2. It's always loose and watery, and it feels like there's still something left inside after I go, if that makes sense.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: No, I don't really feel any of that. I mean, I'm a little anxious about what might be going on, but physically, it's mostly just this gnawing pain and feeling like I need to rush to the bathroom all the time. It's pretty stressful, actually.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, not really. I might get a little hot or warm when I'm in the midst of an episode, but it's not like I'm sweating bullets or anything. And to be honest, the fatigue is probably what's been bothering me just as much as the pain and diarrhea - I've been feeling really exhausted all the time, which is affecting my work and daily life.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain tends to be mostly on my left side, but sometimes it radiates down towards my groin area or feels like it's moving around a bit. It's hard to describe, but it's like this constant gnawing sensation that just won't quit.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it doesn't really radiate outside of my abdominal area. The pain is mostly contained to my lower left side and can spread a bit around my groin, but I don't feel it in my arms, neck, or jaw.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, nothing like that. I mean, I've had some joint pain lately, achy feeling in both knees and ankles, but no numbness or tingling. It's more of a dull ache than anything else, and it doesn't seem to be related to my abdominal symptoms.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been having some trouble sleeping at night because of the diarrhea. It's usually not too bad during the day, but when it hits me in the middle of the night, it wakes me right up and I have to get out of bed to use the bathroom.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, no significant swelling on my ankles or feet. The joint pain is just a dull ache, like I said before - it's not swollen or red, and it doesn't seem to be related to my abdominal symptoms at all.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: No, nothing like that. My feet and ankles just feel a bit achy, like I said before. It's not really affecting my daily activities or anything, it's just this annoying background pain that I'm trying to deal with along with everything else.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: Actually, yeah, there is some nausea and queasiness when I have an episode. It's not always bad, but sometimes it's like my stomach feels really sensitive and I just want to lie down. But with the ibuprofen, that usually helps a bit. And I've been trying to manage it with loperamide, too, as you know, but it doesn't seem to be doing much for me.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, I'd say that's a big part of it. When I'm having an episode, my stomach feels really sensitive and I get this queasy feeling like I might throw up. It's not always bad, but it's definitely uncomfortable. And like I said before, taking ibuprofen helps a bit with the pain, but it doesn't seem to do much for the nausea or diarrhea.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: No, I haven't experienced any fevers, chills, or night sweats. I've been a bit warm and sweaty during some of the episodes, but like I said before, it's not anything too severe. Just a normal body temperature, nothing out of the ordinary. And to be honest, I'm starting to get really concerned about what might be going on with my symptoms. They're just getting worse and worse, and I feel like something more serious is at play.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been having a hard time eating because of the diarrhea and nausea. I'm not really hungry most of the time, and when I do eat, it's usually just something light like crackers or toast. And yeah, I have lost some weight - about 4 kilos (that's around 9 pounds) over the past three weeks. It's been noticeable even to me, which is kind of scary.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient: I'd say my diet has been pretty irregular lately due to my symptoms and long work hours. I've been grabbing takeout or eating at restaurants a lot more often than usual, which isn't great for my stomach. And as for exercise, I used to be pretty active, but with the fatigue and joint pain, it's been hard to find the energy to work out like I used to. My daily routine is pretty hectic - 60+ hour work weeks at a startup can be intense, and I'm usually stressed about meeting deadlines or dealing with work drama.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, my physical activity has taken a hit lately. I used to enjoy playing basketball and hiking on the weekends, but with the fatigue and joint pain, it's been really hard to keep up that level of activity. I've been trying to stick to short walks around my apartment complex or take the stairs at work, but even those small things feel like they're exhausting me more than usual.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's been pretty irregular lately. I've been grabbing takeout or eating at restaurants a lot more often than usual because my symptoms make cooking and eating at home really uncomfortable. Sometimes I'll have a light snack in the morning, maybe some toast or cereal, but lunch and dinner are usually just whatever is convenient - sandwiches, salads, that kind of thing. I've been trying to stick to bland foods, you know, nothing too spicy or fatty, but it's hard when you're eating out all the time. And like I said before, my symptoms have been making me really anxious about food, so even thinking about what to eat can be overwhelming sometimes.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30. It's been tough on her, and I'm worried that I might have a similar issue going on. Other than that, my mom is healthy, but my dad has type 2 diabetes.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: My sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30, and it's been a real concern for me. She's had to deal with some pretty tough symptoms and complications over the years, so I'm worried that I might be looking at something similar. It's hard not to think about it, especially when my own symptoms are getting worse and worse.
